name: HTML and CSS
scope: A BEGINNER in HTML and CSS should understand the basics of HTML as the structure of a webpage and CSS for styling. They should know basic HTML elements like headings, paragraphs, links, images, lists, tables, and forms with attributes like href, src, alt. In CSS, they should understand selectors (tag, .class, #id), basic properties for text styling (color, font-size, text-align), the box model (margin, padding, border, width), and backgrounds (background-color, background-image). They should differentiate between inline, internal, and external CSS, and have a basic grasp of semantic HTML for accessibility and SEO. Tasks include creating static webpages with text, images, and simple forms, applying basic styles, and organizing files with proper linking. Tools they should know include code editors like VS Code or Sublime Text, browsers like Chrome or Firefox, and browser developer tools for debugging. They may have limited exposure to responsive design, Flexbox, Grid, SASS, Bootstrap, or accessibility practices. They should produce clean, readable code, create basic static pages like a personal profile or landing page, and troubleshoot simple issues like broken links or missing attributes. However, they may struggle with cascading, specificity, advanced layouts, or debugging complex CSS. Online resources like MDN and W3Schools should be their primary references.

name: Javascript
scope: A BEGINNER in Javascript can write basic scripts with correct syntax and perform simple tasks like DOM manipulation (e.g., updating text, changing styles) and user interactions (e.g., button clicks). Understands variables (var, let, const), data types, operators, loops (for, while), conditionals (if, else), and simple functions (parameters, return values). Knows basic DOM methods (getElementById, querySelector) and events (click, input), with minimal understanding of event propagation. Uses console.log and browser developer tools for debugging syntax errors and inspecting code. Familiar with simple text editors (e.g., VS Code) but has no exposure to frameworks or advanced JavaScript concepts like closures, async/await, or ES6+. Can write readable, commented code with consistent structure but lacks independent problem-solving, optimization skills, or server-side knowledge (Node.js). Open to learning via tutorials and feedback, with gaps in advanced debugging, frameworks, and performance considerations.
